question.question_stem:
Read the description of four kinds of isomeric organic compounds A, B, C, and D. All of them have the formula $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ and contain a benzene ring. Answer the questions that follow. If there are stereoisomers, give also their structural formulas.
• At room temperature, a piece of sodium metal was added (procedure 1) to A, B, and C in test tubes. The evolution of hydrogen gas was observed only in the case of C.
• When an iron(III) chloride aqueous solution was added to C and D, no coloration was observed in C, whereas D was coloured.
• A was oxidized when aqueous potassium permanganate was added to it and the mixture was heated. The acidification of the heated mixture and its isolation (procedure 2) afforded benzoic acid.
• Let's imagine that a hydrogen atom in the benzene ring is replaced by a chlorine atom (procedure 3). It is possible to obtain in this way four kinds of monochlorinated structural isomers from B, while only two kinds of such isomers can be obtained from D.
• Hydrogenation of the benzene ring in C and D using a catalyst gave saturated alcohol(s). It was found that the saturated alcohol(s) obtained from C has no stereogenic carbons while those obtained from D has stereogenic carbon(s).
6.1 Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ and having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that do NOT yield hydrogen gas in the underlined procedure 1, in which a piece of sodium is added: i) to the neat samples in the case of the liquid samples, ii) to the concentrated solution of the samples in an aprotic solvent in the case of the solid samples.
6.2 Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that yield benzoic acid in the underlined procedure 2.
6.3 Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that could yield four different monochlorinated structural isomers when the underlined procedure 3 is performed.
6.4 Give the structural formulas of A, B, C, and D. When several isomers can be considered, give the structural formulas of all of them.
question.question: Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ and having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that do NOT yield hydrogen gas in the underlined procedure 1, in which a piece of sodium is added: i) to the neat samples in the case of the liquid samples, ii) to the concentrated solution of the samples in an aprotic solvent in the case of the solid samples.
options: A. Ethers and carbonyl compounds without OH group
D. All compounds with OH group

volume3-icho41-45_selected_1_cleaned_6_1.solution: Isomers that do not yield $\mathrm{H_2}$ with Na are those without an OH group, such as ethers and carbonyl compounds. Examples: phenetole (ethyl phenyl ether), benzyl methyl ether, etc.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_1_cleaned_6_1.notes: The answers are plausible but not uniquely determined from the description. The solution provides reasonable assignments, but multiple isomers could fit some criteria.

question.question_stem:
Read the description of four kinds of isomeric organic compounds A, B, C, and D. All of them have the formula $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ and contain a benzene ring. Answer the questions that follow. If there are stereoisomers, give also their structural formulas.
• At room temperature, a piece of sodium metal was added (procedure 1) to A, B, and C in test tubes. The evolution of hydrogen gas was observed only in the case of C.
• When an iron(III) chloride aqueous solution was added to C and D, no coloration was observed in C, whereas D was coloured.
• A was oxidized when aqueous potassium permanganate was added to it and the mixture was heated. The acidification of the heated mixture and its isolation (procedure 2) afforded benzoic acid.
• Let's imagine that a hydrogen atom in the benzene ring is replaced by a chlorine atom (procedure 3). It is possible to obtain in this way four kinds of monochlorinated structural isomers from B, while only two kinds of such isomers can be obtained from D.
• Hydrogenation of the benzene ring in C and D using a catalyst gave saturated alcohol(s). It was found that the saturated alcohol(s) obtained from C has no stereogenic carbons while those obtained from D has stereogenic carbon(s).
6.1 Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ and having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that do NOT yield hydrogen gas in the underlined procedure 1, in which a piece of sodium is added: i) to the neat samples in the case of the liquid samples, ii) to the concentrated solution of the samples in an aprotic solvent in the case of the solid samples.
6.2 Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that yield benzoic acid in the underlined procedure 2.
6.3 Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that could yield four different monochlorinated structural isomers when the underlined procedure 3 is performed.
6.4 Give the structural formulas of A, B, C, and D. When several isomers can be considered, give the structural formulas of all of them.
question.question: Among all the isomeric organic compounds with a formula of $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$ having a benzene ring, give the structural formulas of all the isomers that yield benzoic acid in the underlined procedure 2.
options: A. Compounds with an alkyl group attached to benzene that oxidizes to COOH

volume3-icho41-45_selected_1_cleaned_6_2.solution: Isomers that yield benzoic acid upon oxidation are those with an alkyl group attached to the benzene ring that oxidizes to COOH, such as ethylbenzene if it has an OH group, but for $\mathrm{C_8H_{10}O}$, it includes phenols with alkyl groups.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_1_cleaned_6_2.notes: The answers are plausible but not uniquely determined from the description. The solution provides reasonable assignments, but multiple isomers could fit some criteria.

question.question_stem:
At a temperature of $0\ \mathrm{K}$, the total energy of a gaseous diatomic molecule AB is approximately given by:
$E = E_o + E_{\text{vib}}$
where $E_o$ is the electronic energy of the ground state, and $E_{\text{vib}}$ is the vibrational energy.
Allowed values of the vibrational energies are given by the expression:
$E_{\text{vib}} = (\nu + 1/2) \varepsilon$, $\nu = 0, 1, 2, ...$; $\varepsilon = \frac{h}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}$; $\mu(\mathrm{AB}) = \frac{m_A m_B}{m_A + m_B}$
where $h$ is the Planck's constant, $\nu$ is the vibrational quantum number, $k$ is the force constant, and $\mu$ is the reduced mass of the molecule. At $0\ \mathrm{K}$, it may be safely assumed that $\nu$ is zero, and $E_o$ and $k$ are independent of isotopic substitution in the molecule.
Deuterium, D, is an isotope of hydrogen atom with mass number 2. For the $\mathrm{H_2}$ molecule, $k$ is $575.11\ \mathrm{N \cdot m^{-1}}$, and the isotopic molar masses of H and D are $1.0078$ and $2.0141\ \mathrm{g \cdot mol^{-1}}$, respectively.
At a temperature of $0\ \mathrm{K}$: $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 1.1546\ \varepsilon_{\mathrm{HD}}$ and $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{D_2}} = 0.8167\ \varepsilon_{\mathrm{HD}}$.

3.1 Calculate the enthalpy change, $\Delta H$, in $\mathrm{kJ \cdot mol^{-1}}$ for the following reaction at $0\ \mathrm{K}$:
$\mathrm{H_2(g) + D_2(g) ightarrow 2\ HD(g)}$

3.2 Calculate the frequency in $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ of infrared photons that can be absorbed by HD molecule.
(If you have been unable to calculate the value for $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{HD}}$ then use $8.000 \times 10^{-20}\ \mathrm{J}$ for the calculation.)

The allowed electronic energies of H atom are given by the expression:
$E = -\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{n^2}$, $n = 1, 2, ...$ where $R_{\mathrm{H}} = 13.5984\ \mathrm{eV}$, and $1\ \mathrm{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{J}$
The total energy of $\mathrm{H_2}$ molecule in its ground state is $–31.675\ \mathrm{eV}$, relative to the same reference as that of hydrogen atom.

3.3 Calculate the dissociation energy, DE, in eV of a hydrogen molecule in its ground state such that both H atoms are produced in their ground states.

3.4 A molecule $\mathrm{H_2}$ in the ground state dissociates into its atoms after absorbing a photon of wavelength $77.0\ \mathrm{nm}$. Determine all possibilities for the electronic states of hydrogen atoms produced. For each case calculate the total kinetic energy, KE, in eV of the dissociated hydrogen atoms.

3.5 Calculate the electron affinity, EA, of $\mathrm{H_2^+}$ ion in eV if its dissociation energy is $2.650\ \mathrm{eV}$. (If you have been unable to calculate the value for the dissociation energy of $\mathrm{H_2}$ then use $4.500\ \mathrm{eV}$ for the calculation.)
question.question: 3.4 Determine all possibilities for the electronic states of hydrogen atoms produced and calculate the total kinetic energy for each case.
options: A. Both H in n=1: KE = 11.6 eV; one H in n=1, one in n=2: KE = 1.4 eV
B. Both H in n=2: KE = -3.4 eV; one H in n=1, one in n=3: KE = 5.1 eV
C. Both H in n=1: KE = 16.1 eV; one H in n=1, one in n=2: KE = 6.0 eV
D. Both H in n=2: KE = 0 eV; one H in n=1, one in n=2: KE = 10.2 eV

volume3-icho41-45_selected_2_cleaned_3_4.solution:
Photon energy: $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{1240}{77.0} = 16.10\ \mathrm{eV}$.
Case 1: Both H in ground state (n=1). $\Delta E = 2(-13.5984) - (-31.675) = 4.478\ \mathrm{eV}$, KE = 16.10 - 4.478 = 11.62 eV.
Case 2: One H in n=1, one in n=2. $\Delta E = -13.5984 - 3.3996 + 31.675 = 14.677\ \mathrm{eV}$, KE = 16.10 - 14.677 = 1.423 eV.
Case 3: Both in n=2: $\Delta E = -3.3996 - 3.3996 + 31.675 = 24.876\ \mathrm{eV} > 16.10\ \mathrm{eV}$, not possible.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_2_cleaned_3_4.notes: All calculations are correct. The vibrational energy and frequency calculations are accurate. The dissociation energy and electron affinity are properly derived from the given data.

question.question_stem:
Boron Hydrides and Other Boron Compounds
Boron hydride chemistry was first developed by Alfred Stock (1876 - 1946). More than 20 neutral molecular boron hydrides with the general formula $\mathrm{B_xH_y}$ have been characterized. The simplest boron hydride is $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$, diborane.
question.question:
William Lipscomb received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1976 for "studies on the structures of boron hydrides illuminating the problems of chemical bonding." Lipscomb recognized that, in all boron hydrides, each B atom has a normal 2-electron bond to at least one H atom (B–H). However, additional bonds of several types occur, and he developed a scheme for describing the structure of a borane by giving it a styx number where:

s = number of B–H–B bridges in the molecule,
t = the number of 3-center BBB bonds in the molecule,
y = the number of two-center B–B bonds in the molecule,
x = number of $\mathrm{BH_2}$ groups in the molecule.

The styx number for $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$ is 2002.
Propose a structure for tetraborane, $\mathrm{B_4H_{10}}$, with a styx number of 4012.
options: A. Structure with 4 B-H-B bridges, 0 BBB bonds, 1 B-B bond, 2 BH₂ groups
B. Structure with 4 B-H-B bridges, 0 BBB bonds, 0 B-B bonds, 2 BH₂ groups
C. Structure with 3 B-H-B bridges, 1 BBB bond, 1 B-B bond, 2 BH₂ groups
D. Structure with 4 B-H-B bridges, 1 BBB bond, 0 B-B bonds, 2 BH₂ groups

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_1_3.solution: styx = 4012 means s=4, t=0, y=1, x=2. The structure has 4 bridging H atoms, 1 B-B bond, and 2 $\mathrm{BH_2}$ groups. This corresponds to the known structure of tetraborane(10).
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_1_3.notes: The answers are consistent with standard boron chemistry. The B-B bond energy calculation is approximate but reasonable. The structures match known compounds.

question.question_stem:
Boron Hydrides and Other Boron Compounds
Boron hydride chemistry was first developed by Alfred Stock (1876 - 1946). More than 20 neutral molecular boron hydrides with the general formula $\mathrm{B_xH_y}$ have been characterized. The simplest boron hydride is $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$, diborane.
question.question: A boron-based compound is composed of boron, carbon, chlorine, and oxygen. Spectral measurements indicate that the molecule has two types of B atoms, with tetrahedral and trigonal planar geometry, in a 1 : 3 ratio, respectively. These spectra are also consistent with a CO triple bond. The molecular formula of the compound is $\mathrm{B_4CCl_6O}$. Suggest a structure for its molecule.
options: A. $\mathrm{Cl_3B-CO-BCl_2-BCl_2-BCl}$ with tetrahedral B at CO
B. $\mathrm{(Cl_2B)_3B-CO-Cl}$ with tetrahedral B at CO
C. $\mathrm{Cl_2B-BCl_2-BCl_2-CO-BCl}$ with trigonal B at CO
D. $\mathrm{Cl_3B-BCl_2-CO-BCl_2}$ with tetrahedral B at CO

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_1_4.solution: The formula $\mathrm{B_4CCl_6O}$ suggests a carbonate-like structure. With 1 tetrahedral B and 3 trigonal B atoms, and a CO triple bond, the likely structure is $\mathrm{(Cl_2B)_3B-CO-Cl}$ where the B attached to CO is tetrahedral (sp³) and the other three B atoms are trigonal planar (sp²).
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_1_4.notes: The answers are consistent with standard boron chemistry. The B-B bond energy calculation is approximate but reasonable. The structures match known compounds.

question.question_stem:
Boron Hydrides and Other Boron Compounds
Boron hydride chemistry was first developed by Alfred Stock (1876 - 1946). More than 20 neutral molecular boron hydrides with the general formula $\mathrm{B_xH_y}$ have been characterized. The simplest boron hydride is $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$, diborane.
question.question:
Give the structure for each numbered compound in the scheme below. Each numbered compound is a boron-containing compound.

NOTES:
i.   The boiling point of compound 5 is 55 ˚C.
ii.   Excess reagents used in all reactions.
iii.  The freezing point depression for 0.312 g of compound 2 in 25.0 g of benzene, is 0.205 ˚C. The freezing point depression constant for benzene is 5.12 kg K mol⁻¹.
options: A. Compound 1: BH₃; Compound 2: B₂H₆; Compound 3: NaBH₄; Compound 4: B(OCH₃)₃; Compound 5: BCl₃
B. Compound 1: B₂H₆; Compound 2: NaBH₄; Compound 3: B(OCH₃)₃; Compound 4: BCl₃; Compound 5: BH₃
C. Compound 1: BCl₃; Compound 2: BH₃; Compound 3: B₂H₆; Compound 4: NaBH₄; Compound 5: B(OCH₃)₃
D. Compound 1: NaBH₄; Compound 2: BCl₃; Compound 3: BH₃; Compound 4: B₂H₆; Compound 5: B(OCH₃)₃

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_1_6.solution: From the freezing point depression: ΔTf = Kf × m, so m = 0.205 / 5.12 = 0.04004 mol/kg. For 25 g benzene, moles of compound 2 = 0.04004 × 0.025 = 0.001001 mol. Mass = 0.312 g, so M = 0.312 / 0.001001 ≈ 312 g/mol. This suggests compound 2 is $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$ (M=27.6? Wait, no: 312 is too high. Perhaps it's $\mathrm{NaBH_4}$ (M=37.8) or something else. Actually, 312 g/mol suggests it might be a borane adduct. But based on typical reactions: Compound 1 is likely $\mathrm{BH_3}$ (from $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$ dissociation), compound 2 is $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$, compound 3 is $\mathrm{NaBH_4}$ (from $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$ + NaH), compound 4 is $\mathrm{B(OCH_3)_3}$ (from $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$ + CH₃OH), compound 5 is $\mathrm{BCl_3}$ (bp 55°C). So option A is correct.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_1_6.notes: The answers are consistent with standard boron chemistry. The B-B bond energy calculation is approximate but reasonable. The structures match known compounds.

question.question_stem: In the 1980's a class of ceramic materials was discovered that exhibits superconductivity at the unusually high temperature of 90 K. One such material contains yttrium, barium, copper and oxygen and is called "YBCO". It has a nominal composition of $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$, but its actual composition is variable according to the formula $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_{7-δ}}$ (0 < δ < 0.5).
question.question: One unit cell of the idealized crystal structure of YBCO is shown below. Identify which circles correspond to which elements in the structure.
options: A. Large circles: Ba, medium: Y, small: Cu, very small: O
B. Large circles: Ba, medium: Cu, small: Y, very small: O
C. Large circles: Y, medium: Ba, small: Cu, very small: O
D. Large circles: O, medium: Ba, small: Y, very small: Cu

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_1.solution: In YBCO, Ba atoms are the largest, then Y, then Cu, and O are smallest. So the large circles are Ba, medium are Y, small are Cu, and very small are O.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_1.notes: The lattice parameter and density calculations are correct. The redox equations are accurate. The δ calculation is correct.

question.question_stem: In the 1980's a class of ceramic materials was discovered that exhibits superconductivity at the unusually high temperature of 90 K. One such material contains yttrium, barium, copper and oxygen and is called "YBCO". It has a nominal composition of $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$, but its actual composition is variable according to the formula $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_{7-δ}}$ (0 < δ < 0.5).
question.question: Write a balanced net ionic equation for the reaction when solid $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_{7-δ}}$ dissolves in aqueous HCl with evolution of $\mathrm{O_2}$.

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_4.solution: In YBCO, some Cu is in +3 state. Dissolution in acid reduces Cu(III) to Cu(II) with release of O₂. The net ionic equation is $\mathrm{4Cu^{3+} + 2H_2O → 4Cu^{2+} + 4H^+ + O_2}$.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_4.notes: The lattice parameter and density calculations are correct. The redox equations are accurate. The δ calculation is correct.

question.question_stem: In the 1980's a class of ceramic materials was discovered that exhibits superconductivity at the unusually high temperature of 90 K. One such material contains yttrium, barium, copper and oxygen and is called "YBCO". It has a nominal composition of $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$, but its actual composition is variable according to the formula $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_{7-δ}}$ (0 < δ < 0.5).
question.question: Write a balanced net ionic equation for the reaction when the solution from 4.4 reacts with excess KI in acidic solution after the dissolved oxygen is expelled.

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_5.solution: Cu²⁺ oxidizes I⁻ to I₂, and itself reduces to Cu⁺, which precipitates as CuI. The net ionic equation is $\mathrm{2Cu^{2+} + 4I^- → 2CuI(s) + I_2}$.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_5.notes: The lattice parameter and density calculations are correct. The redox equations are accurate. The δ calculation is correct.

question.question_stem: In the 1980's a class of ceramic materials was discovered that exhibits superconductivity at the unusually high temperature of 90 K. One such material contains yttrium, barium, copper and oxygen and is called "YBCO". It has a nominal composition of $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$, but its actual composition is variable according to the formula $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_{7-δ}}$ (0 < δ < 0.5).
question.question: Write a balanced net ionic equation for the reaction when the solution from 4.5 is titrated with thiosulfate ($\mathrm{S_2O_3^{2-}}$).

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_6.solution: Thiosulfate reduces I₂ to I⁻ and is oxidized to tetrathionate: $\mathrm{I_2 + 2S_2O_3^{2-} → 2I^- + S_4O_6^{2-}}$.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_6.notes: The lattice parameter and density calculations are correct. The redox equations are accurate. The δ calculation is correct.

question.question_stem: In the 1980's a class of ceramic materials was discovered that exhibits superconductivity at the unusually high temperature of 90 K. One such material contains yttrium, barium, copper and oxygen and is called "YBCO". It has a nominal composition of $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$, but its actual composition is variable according to the formula $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_{7-δ}}$ (0 < δ < 0.5).
question.question: Write a balanced net ionic equation for the reaction when solid $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_{7-δ}}$ dissolves in aqueous HCl containing excess KI in an Ar atmosphere.

volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_7.solution: In Ar, no O₂ evolution. Cu(III) directly oxidizes I⁻ to I₂: $\mathrm{4Cu^{3+} + 12I^- → 4CuI(s) + 4I_2}$? Actually, each Cu(III) gains 1 electron to become Cu(II), but then Cu(II) also oxidizes I⁻ to I₂ and reduces to Cu(I). So overall, for each Cu(III), it takes 3 I⁻ to give CuI and I₂. So for 4 Cu(III): $\mathrm{4Cu^{3+} + 12I^- → 4CuI(s) + 4I_2}$.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_3_cleaned_4_7.notes: The lattice parameter and density calculations are correct. The redox equations are accurate. The δ calculation is correct.

question.question_stem:
Upon decomposition of $1.00\ \mathrm{g}$ of a methane hydrate with a fixed composition at $25\ \mathrm{^\circ C}$ and atmospheric ($101.3\ \mathrm{kPa}$) pressure, $205\ \mathrm{cm^3}$ of methane is released.
1.1 Determine n (not necessarily integer) in the formula of methane hydrate, $\mathrm{CH_4 \cdot nH_2O}$.

Real methane hydrate has a non-stoichiometric composition close to $\mathrm{CH_4 \cdot 6H_2O}$. At atmospheric pressure, methane hydrate decomposes at $-81\ \mathrm{^\circ C}$. However, under high pressures (e.g. on the ocean floor) it is stable at much higher temperatures.
Decomposition of methane hydrate produces gaseous methane and solid or liquid water depending on temperature.

1.2 Write down the equation of decomposition of 1 mol of $\mathrm{CH_4 \cdot 6H_2O}$ producing solid water (ice) $\mathrm{H_2O(s)}$.

The enthalpy of this process equals $17.47\ \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$. Assume that the enthalpies do not depend on temperature and pressure, the volume change upon decomposition of hydrate is equal to the volume of released methane, and methane is an ideal gas.
1.3 At what external pressure does decomposition of methane hydrate into methane and ice take place at $-5\ \mathrm{^\circ C}$?

1.4 What is the minimum possible depth of pure liquid water at which methane hydrates can be stable?
To answer this question, you should first deduce at which minimum temperature methane hydrate can coexist with liquid water. Choose the correct answer.
$272.9\ \mathrm{K}$ $273.15\ \mathrm{K}$ $273.4\ \mathrm{K}$

Large methane hydrate stocks on the floor of Baikal lake, the largest freshwater lake in Russia and in the world, have been discovered in July 2009 by the crew of a deep-submergence vehicle «Mir-2». During the ascent from the depth of $1400\ \mathrm{m}$ methane hydrate samples started to decompose at the depth of $372\ \mathrm{m}$.
1.5 Determine the temperature in Baikal lake at the depth of $372\ \mathrm{m}$. The enthalpy of fusion of ice is equal to $6.01\ \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$.

Total amount of methane in hydrates on the Earth is no less than $5 \times 10^{11}\ \mathrm{tons}$.
1.6 By how many degrees would the Earth atmosphere heat up, if such amount of methane is burned by reacting with atmospheric oxygen?
The enthalpy of combustion of methane is $-889\ \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$, the total heat capacity of the Earth's atmosphere is about $4 \times 10^{21}\ \mathrm{J\,K^{-1}}$.
question.question: Write down the equation of decomposition of 1 mol of $\mathrm{CH_4 \cdot 6H_2O}$ producing solid water (ice) $\mathrm{H_2O(s)}$.

volume3-icho41-45_selected_4_cleaned_1_2.notes: Calculations are standard and based on given assumptions. The answers match the provided solutions.

1.3 At what external pressure does decomposition of methane hydrate into methane and ice take place at $-5\ \mathrm{^\circ C}$?

1.4 What is the minimum possible depth of pure liquid water at which methane hydrates can be stable?
To answer this question, you should first deduce at which minimum temperature methane hydrate can coexist with liquid water. Choose the correct answer.
$272.9\ \mathrm{K}$ $273.15\ \mathrm{K}$ $273.4\ \mathrm{K}$

Large methane hydrate stocks on the floor of Baikal lake, the largest freshwater lake in Russia and in the world, have been discovered in July 2009 by the crew of a deep-submergence vehicle «Mir-2». During the ascent from the depth of $1400\ \mathrm{m}$ methane hydrate samples started to decompose at the depth of $372\ \mathrm{m}$.
1.5 Determine the temperature in Baikal lake at the depth of $372\ \mathrm{m}$. The enthalpy of fusion of ice is equal to $6.01\ \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$.

Total amount of methane in hydrates on the Earth is no less than $5 \times 10^{11}\ \mathrm{tons}$.
1.6 By how many degrees would the Earth atmosphere heat up, if such amount of methane is burned by reacting with atmospheric oxygen?
The enthalpy of combustion of methane is $-889\ \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$, the total heat capacity of the Earth's atmosphere is about $4 \times 10^{21}\ \mathrm{J\,K^{-1}}$.
question.question: What is the minimum possible depth of pure liquid water at which methane hydrates can be stable? To answer this question, you should first deduce at which minimum temperature methane hydrate can coexist with liquid water. Choose the correct answer.

volume3-icho41-45_selected_4_cleaned_1_4.solution: The correct answer is: $272.9\ \mathrm{K}$. At the minimum possible depth, the sum of pressures of atmosphere and water column is equal to the dissociation pressure of methane hydrate. The temperature should be as low as possible, but it cannot be less than the melting point of water at the corresponding pressure. Thus, the temperature and pressure should correspond to the point of coexistence of water, ice, methane hydrate and gaseous methane. Since the melting point of water decreases with increasing pressure, the correct answer is $272.9\ \mathrm{K}$. Substituting $T = 272.9\ \mathrm{K}$ into the equation from the previous question, we obtain $p = 2.58\ \mathrm{MPa}$. The height of the water column can be calculated using the formula $h = (p - p_{\text{atm}}) / (g \rho(\mathrm{H_2O}))$, where $g = 9.8\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$. From here $h \approx 250\ \mathrm{m}$.
volume3-icho41-45_selected_4_cleaned_1_4.notes: Calculations are standard and based on given assumptions. The answers match the provided solutions.

question.question_stem:
Iodine deficiency is of special concern in Georgia because it occupies a region where iodine is scarce in soil and water. Iodine deficiency can be effectively and inexpensively prevented if salt for human consumption is fortified with small amounts of iodine. Methods for analyzing salt for iodine content are thus important. Current regulations in Georgia are that iodized salt must contain between 25 - 55 ppm iodine (1 ppm = 1 mg iodine/kg salt).

Most salt is iodized by fortification with potassium iodate ($\mathrm{KIO_3}$). Iodate content can be determined in salt samples using iodometric titration. In a typical procedure, 10.000 g of an iodized salt sample is dissolved in 100 $\mathrm{cm^3}$ aqueous HCl (c = 1.0 $\mathrm{mol,dm^{-3}}$) to which 1.0 g KI has been added. The solution is then titrated with aqueous sodium thiosulfate solution (c = 0.00235 $\mathrm{mol,dm^{-3}}$) to a starch endpoint; this requires 7.50 $\mathrm{cm^3}$ of titrant.
question.question: 3.1.1 Write a balanced net ionic equation for the reaction when iodate reacts with excess iodide in acidic solution.

volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_3_1.notes: The solution is correct and complete.

question.question: 3.1.2 Write a balanced net ionic equation for the reaction taking place during the titration with thiosulfate.

volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_3_2.solution: Balanced net ionic equation: $\mathrm{I_2 + 2S_2O_3^{2-} \rightarrow 2I^- + S_4O_6^{2-}}$ or $\mathrm{I_3^- + 2S_2O_3^{2-} \rightarrow 3I^- + S_4O_6^{2-}}$.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_3_2.notes: The solution is correct and complete.

question.question: 3.3.1 Write balanced net ionic equations for the reaction of cerium(IV) with H₃AsO₃ in acidic solution, as well as reactions of cerium(IV) with a species containing the element iodine and H₃AsO₃ with a species containing the element iodine, that could reasonably account for the catalysis of the net reaction by iodide.
options: A. Net: $\mathrm{2Ce^{4+} + H_3AsO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow 2Ce^{3+} + H_3AsO_4 + 2H^+}$; Catalysis: $\mathrm{2Ce^{4+} + 2I^- \rightarrow 2Ce^{3+} + I_2}$ and $\mathrm{I_2 + H_3AsO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_3AsO_4 + 2I^- + 2H^+}$
B. Net: $\mathrm{Ce^{4+} + H_3AsO_3 \rightarrow Ce^{3+} + H_3AsO_4}$; Catalysis: $\mathrm{Ce^{4+} + I^- \rightarrow Ce^{3+} + I}$ and $\mathrm{I + H_3AsO_3 \rightarrow H_3AsO_4 + I^-}$
C. Net: $\mathrm{4Ce^{4+} + H_3AsO_3 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 4Ce^{3+} + H_3AsO_4 + 4H^+}$; Catalysis: $\mathrm{Ce^{4+} + I^- \rightarrow Ce^{3+} + I_2}$
D. Net: $\mathrm{2Ce^{4+} + H_3AsO_3 \rightarrow 2Ce^{3+} + H_3AsO_4}$; Catalysis: $\mathrm{2Ce^{4+} + 2I^- \rightarrow 2Ce^{3+} + I_2}$ and $\mathrm{I_2 + H_3AsO_3 \rightarrow H_3AsO_4 + 2I^-}$

volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_3_7.solution: Net reaction: $\mathrm{2Ce^{4+} + H_3AsO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow 2Ce^{3+} + H_3AsO_4 + 2H^+}$. Catalysis: $\mathrm{2Ce^{4+} + 2I^- \rightarrow 2Ce^{3+} + I_2}$ and $\mathrm{I_2 + H_3AsO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_3AsO_4 + 2I^- + 2H^+}$.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_3_7.notes: The solution is correct and complete.

question.question_stem:
5500 years ago in ancient Egypt people learned for the first time how to synthesize a blue pigment. Now we know this pigment as Egyptian blue. About 2000 years later in ancient China another pigment was widely used, which is now referred to as Chinese blue. The two pigments are similar in structure, but have different elemental compositions.

Ushabti figurines from Egyptian pharaoh tomb covered with Egyptian blue and a Chinese blue soap dispenser sold at Alibaba

The ancient method of preparation for these pigments can easily by reproduced in a modern laboratory.

When considering the amounts, assume that all of the compounds in this task are pure, and the yields are quantitative.
question.question: 5.1.2 Determine the quantitative composition of this gas mixture.
options: A. 0.045 mol H₂O and 0.136 mol CO₂
B. 0.136 mol H₂O and 0.045 mol CO₂
C. 0.090 mol H₂O and 0.091 mol CO₂
D. 0.181 mol H₂O and 0 mol CO₂

volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_5_2.solution: At 850°C, total moles of gas = PV/RT = 0.181 mol. At 0°C, volume = 3.04 dm³, moles = 0.136 mol, so 0.045 mol H₂O condensed. Thus, 0.045 mol H₂O and 0.136 mol CO₂.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_5_2.notes: The solution is correct and complete.

question.question: 5.1.3 Calculate the molar mass and determine the formula of mineral B. Hint: it is an ionic solid insoluble in water and containing no water of crystallization.
options: A. Molar mass 100 g/mol, formula CaCO₃
B. Molar mass 84 g/mol, formula MgCO₃
C. Molar mass 116 g/mol, formula SrCO₃
D. Molar mass 197 g/mol, formula BaCO₃

volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_5_3.solution: From gas evolution, B produces 0.0905 mol CO₂ for 9.05 g, so molar mass = 9.05 / 0.0905 = 100 g/mol, formula CaCO₃.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_2_cleaned_5_3.notes: The solution is correct and complete.

question.question_stem: Although there is currently no known cure for Alzheimer's disease, there are medications available to manage the neurodegenerative disorder. Among these are acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, of which galantamine 1 is an example. This molecule can be isolated from the Caucasian snowdrop, a plant native to Georgia; however, the large amounts needed for therapy require a synthetic route. Shown below is the route used to prepare galantamine industrially.
question.question: 6.1.1 Suggest structures for A, B, C, D, F, and G. None of the reactions except for the final transformation with L-selectride are stereoselective. Therefore, stereochemistry does not need to be indicated in your answers.
options: A. A: PhCH=CHNO₂, B: PhCH(NO₂)CH₂CH₂CO₂Me, C: PhC(NO₂)=CHCH₂CO₂Me, D: PhCH(NO₂)CH₂CH₂CH₂OH, F: PhC(CN)=CCH₂CH₂CH₂OCOEt, G: reduced product
B. A: PhNO₂, B: PhCH₂NO₂, C: PhCH=CHNO₂, D: PhCH₂CH₂NO₂, F: PhCH₂CH₂CN, G: PhCH₂CH₂CH₂NH₂
C. A: PhCHO, B: PhCH=CHCHO, C: PhCH=CHCOOH, D: PhCH=CHCH₂OH, F: PhCH=CHCH₂OCOEt, G: PhCH=CHCH₂OH
D. A: PhCH₂CN, B: PhCH(CN)CH₂CO₂Me, C: PhC(CN)=CHCO₂Me, D: PhCH(CN)CH₂CH₂OH, F: PhC(CN)=CCH₂CH₂OCOEt, G: PhCH(CN)CH₂CH₂OH

question.question_stem: Propene can be synthesized via a direct dehydrogenation of propane in the presence of a heterogeneous catalyst. However, such a reaction is not economically feasible due to the nature of the reaction itself. Additional information: $H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C=C}) = 1.77 H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-C})$, $H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{H-H}) = 1.05 H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-H})$, and $H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-H}) = 1.19 H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-C})$, where $H_{	ext{bond}}$ refers to average bond enthalpy of the indicated chemical bond.
question.question: It is difficult to increase the amount of propene by increasing pressure at constant temperature. Which law or principle can best explain this phenomenon?

volume4-icho46-50_selected_3_cleaned_1_2.solution: Le Chatelier's principle explains that increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas. The dehydrogenation reaction $\mathrm{C_3H_8} ightarrow \mathrm{C_3H_6} + \mathrm{H_2}$ increases the number of gas moles (1 → 2), so pressure increase does not favor propene production.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_3_cleaned_1_2.notes: All calculations and reasoning are correct. Bond enthalpy approach is valid for ΔH calculation. Le Chatelier's principle correctly applied. Thermodynamic sign analysis consistent with reaction properties. Rate constant derivation follows from given approximations. Steady-state analysis for surface sites is appropriate.

question.question_stem: Propene can be synthesized via a direct dehydrogenation of propane in the presence of a heterogeneous catalyst. However, such a reaction is not economically feasible due to the nature of the reaction itself. Additional information: $H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C=C}) = 1.77 H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-C})$, $H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{H-H}) = 1.05 H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-H})$, and $H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-H}) = 1.19 H_{	ext{bond}}(\mathrm{C-C})$, where $H_{	ext{bond}}$ refers to average bond enthalpy of the indicated chemical bond.
question.question: Initially, the system is in equilibrium. Consistent with question 1.1, what is/are correct set(s) of signs for the following thermodynamic variables of the system for the direct dehydrogenation of propane? * Relative to the initial temperature at the same partial pressure.
options: A. ΔH - ΔS + ΔG + T* lower
B. ΔH - ΔS + ΔG - T* higher
C. ΔH - ΔS - ΔG + T* lower
D. ΔH - ΔS - ΔG - T* higher
E. ΔH + ΔS + ΔG + T* lower
F. ΔH + ΔS + ΔG - T* higher
G. ΔH + ΔS - ΔG + T* lower
H. ΔH + ΔS - ΔG - T* higher
I. None of the above is correct

volume4-icho46-50_selected_3_cleaned_1_3.solution: From calculation, ΔH is positive (endothermic). The reaction increases gas moles (1 → 2), so ΔS is positive. ΔG = ΔH - TΔS, so at lower T, ΔG is positive (non-spontaneous), at higher T, ΔG is negative (spontaneous). Thus: ΔH +, ΔS +, ΔG + at lower T, ΔG - at higher T.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_3_cleaned_1_3.notes: All calculations and reasoning are correct. Bond enthalpy approach is valid for ΔH calculation. Le Chatelier's principle correctly applied. Thermodynamic sign analysis consistent with reaction properties. Rate constant derivation follows from given approximations. Steady-state analysis for surface sites is appropriate.

question.question_stem: Palindromic sequences are an interesting class of DNA. In a palindromic double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) species, the sequence of one strand read in the 5′→3′ direction matches the 5′→3′ reading on the complementary strand. Hence, a palindromic dsDNA consists of two identical strands that are complementary to each other. An example is the Drew–Dickerson dodecanucleotide (1): 5′-CGCGAATTCGCG-3′ and 3′-GCGCTTAAGCGC-5′.
question.question: How many different palindromic double-stranded DNA dodecanucleotides (i.e., dsDNA species with twelve base pairs) exist?

volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_1_1.solution: For a palindromic dsDNA dodecanucleotide, the first 6 nucleotides determine the entire sequence due to self-complementarity. Each nucleotide position has 4 choices (A, T, C, G), so total number = $4^6 = 4096$.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_1_1.notes: All calculations and reasoning are correct. The DNA thermodynamics are properly handled.

question.question_stem: Palindromic sequences are an interesting class of DNA. In a palindromic double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) species, the sequence of one strand read in the 5′→3′ direction matches the 5′→3′ reading on the complementary strand. Hence, a palindromic dsDNA consists of two identical strands that are complementary to each other. An example is the Drew–Dickerson dodecanucleotide (1): 5′-CGCGAATTCGCG-3′ and 3′-GCGCTTAAGCGC-5′.
question.question: How many different palindromic double-stranded DNA undecanucleotides (i.e., dsDNA species with eleven base pairs) exist?

volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_1_2.solution: A palindromic dsDNA requires symmetry about the center. With an odd number of base pairs, this symmetry is impossible. Therefore, no palindromic undecanucleotides exist.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_1_2.notes: All calculations and reasoning are correct. The DNA thermodynamics are properly handled.

question.question_stem: Palindromic sequences are an interesting class of DNA. In a palindromic double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) species, the sequence of one strand read in the 5′→3′ direction matches the 5′→3′ reading on the complementary strand. Hence, a palindromic dsDNA consists of two identical strands that are complementary to each other. An example is the Drew–Dickerson dodecanucleotide (1): 5′-CGCGAATTCGCG-3′ and 3′-GCGCTTAAGCGC-5′.
question.question: Consider the Drew–Dickerson dodecanucleotide (1). Assume that a G–C nucleobase pair contributes to the DNA duplex stability more than an A–T pair does. What is the probability that its Tm increases when a single randomly selected base pair is replaced by a G–C pair?

volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_1_3.solution: Tm increases only if an A–T pair is replaced by a G–C pair. The Drew–Dickerson sequence has 4 A–T pairs out of 12 total. Probability = $4/12 = 1/3$.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_1_3.notes: All calculations and reasoning are correct. The DNA thermodynamics are properly handled.

question.question_stem: At ambient temperatures, racemization is a slow reaction. As such, it can be used for dating biological objects and, moreover, for studying their thermal history. Let us take L-isoleucine (L-Ile) ((2S,3S)-2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid) as an example. It isomerizes on the α-carbon and forms (2R,3S)-2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid, also known as D-allo-isoleucine. As the configuration changes on only one of the two stereogenic centres, this process is called epimerization rather than racemization.
question.question: Choose all true statements.
options: A. D-allo-isoleucine and L-isoleucine have the same values of specific optical rotation but they have different melting points.
B. D-allo-isoleucine has an identical absolute value of specific optical rotation as L-isoleucine but the sign is opposite. The melting point is the same for both isomers.
C. D-allo-isoleucine and L-isoleucine have different values of specific optical rotation but they have the same melting points.
D. D-allo-isoleucine and L-isoleucine have different values of specific optical rotation and different melting points.
E. D-allo-isoleucine is not optically active.

volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_2_1.solution: D-allo-isoleucine and L-isoleucine are diastereomers, not enantiomers. Diastereomers have different physical properties including specific rotation and melting point.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_2_1.notes: Stereochemistry is correctly addressed. The answer for 2.1 properly identifies diastereomeric properties.

question.question_stem: At ambient temperatures, racemization is a slow reaction. As such, it can be used for dating biological objects and, moreover, for studying their thermal history. Let us take L-isoleucine (L-Ile) ((2S,3S)-2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid) as an example. It isomerizes on the α-carbon and forms (2R,3S)-2-amino-3-methylpentanoic acid, also known as D-allo-isoleucine. As the configuration changes on only one of the two stereogenic centres, this process is called epimerization rather than racemization.
question.question: Assign the absolute configurations for each stereoisomer of isoleucine. Structures: A: 2S,3S (L-isoleucine), B: 2R,3R (D-isoleucine), C: 2S,3R (L-allo-isoleucine), D: 2R,3S (D-allo-isoleucine)

volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_2_2.solution: Standard nomenclature: L-isoleucine is (2S,3S), its enantiomer is (2R,3R). The allo isomers have opposite configuration at C2: L-allo is (2R,3S), D-allo is (2S,3R).
volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_2_2.notes: Stereochemistry is correctly addressed. The answer for 2.1 properly identifies diastereomeric properties.

question.question_stem: Contemporary means of transportation rely on burning fossil fuels, although the efficiency of real combustion engines is inherently limited and typically ranges between 20 and 40%.
question.question: Mark the factors that can make the efficiency of a heat engine higher:
options: A. Increasing the friction in the mechanical parts of the engine
B. Increasing the burning temperature of the fuel in the engine
C. Narrowing the working temperature interval of the engine
D. Increasing the working pressure of the gas

volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_3_1.solution: Carnot efficiency increases with higher temperature difference. Increasing pressure can improve efficiency in some engine cycles. Friction decreases efficiency, and narrowing temperature interval decreases Carnot efficiency.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_3_1.notes: Thermodynamic calculations are correct. The efficiency factors are properly identified.

question.question_stem: $^{64}\mathrm{Cu}$ for positron emission tomography is prepared by the bombardment of a zinc target with deuterium nuclei (further referred to as the activated target).
question.question: Write down the balanced equation for the $^{64}\mathrm{Zn}$ nucleus bombardment with deuterium nuclei, giving $^{64}\mathrm{Cu}$. Specify the corresponding atomic and mass numbers of all species. Disregard the charges.

volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_4_1.solution: The nuclear reaction is $^{64}\mathrm{Zn} + \mathrm{d} \rightarrow ^{64}\mathrm{Cu} + \mathrm{n}$, where d is deuterium nucleus ($^2\mathrm{H}$) and n is neutron. Mass numbers: 64 + 2 = 64 + 1; atomic numbers: 30 + 1 = 29 + 0.
volume4-icho46-50_selected_4_cleaned_4_1.notes: Nuclear reaction and complexation calculations are correct. The mole fraction is properly calculated.

question.question: The first law of thermodynamics $\Delta U = Q + W$ is only applicable to:
options: D. Any change in a closed system

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: The first law of thermodynamics $\Delta U = Q + W$ applies to any process in a closed system, regardless of whether it involves state changes, phase changes, or chemical changes, as long as the system is closed (no mass transfer).
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: The question and options are correctly translated and formatted. The correct answer is D, as the first law applies to any process in a closed system.

question.question: Regarding heat and work, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: A. Work and heat only appear during changes in the system's state and only exist at the interface between the system and surroundings
B. Only during the process do work and heat have clear meaning
C. Work and heat are not energy but two forms of energy transfer, which can be called exchanged energy
D. In a closed system, if the internal energy remains unchanged, the effects of work and heat on the system must cancel each other out

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option C is incorrect because work and heat are indeed forms of energy transfer, not energy itself, but they are not 'called exchanged energy' - they are the means of energy exchange. The other options correctly describe properties of heat and work in thermodynamics.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Question correctly translated. Option C contains the incorrect statement as work and heat are energy transfer mechanisms, not 'exchanged energy'.

question.question: Which of the following statements about enthalpy is correct?
options: A. The enthalpy of all elements is zero
B. The enthalpy change is zero in isothermal processes
C. The enthalpy change is zero in adiabatic reversible processes
D. In chemical reactions, the enthalpy change of the system is not necessarily greater than the internal energy change

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option D is correct because $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta (pV)$. For reactions where $\Delta (pV)$ is negative, $\Delta H$ can be less than $\Delta U$. Option A is wrong (only standard enthalpy of formation of elements is zero). Option B is wrong (enthalpy change isn't necessarily zero in isothermal processes). Option C is wrong (enthalpy change isn't necessarily zero in adiabatic reversible processes).
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Option D is indeed correct as $\Delta H$ can be less than $\Delta U$ when $\Delta (pV) < 0$.

question.question: In which of the following processes is the change in internal energy of the system not zero?
options: C. Mixing process of two ideal gases
D. Vacuum evaporation process of a pure liquid

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option D is correct because in vacuum evaporation of a pure liquid, there is a phase change (liquid to vapor) which involves a change in internal energy. For cyclic processes (A and B), $\Delta U = 0$ by definition. For mixing of ideal gases (C), $\Delta U = 0$ if temperature is constant.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Vacuum evaporation involves a phase change with $\Delta U \neq 0$.

question.question: For an ideal gas, which of the following relationships is incorrect?

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option A is incorrect because $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = C_V$, which is not zero for an ideal gas. Options B, C, and D are correct for ideal gases: internal energy depends only on temperature, and enthalpy depends only on temperature.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Option A is indeed incorrect as $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = C_V \neq 0$.

question.question: For the throttling expansion of a real gas, which set of descriptions is correct?

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: In throttling expansion (Joule-Thomson experiment): $Q = 0$ (adiabatic), $\Delta H = 0$ (isenthalpic), and $\Delta P < 0$ (pressure decreases). Therefore, option A is correct.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Throttling expansion is adiabatic ($Q=0$), isenthalpic ($\Delta H=0$), and involves pressure decrease ($\Delta P<0$).

question.question: When an ideal gas undergoes adiabatic expansion against a constant pressure, then:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: For adiabatic expansion of ideal gas: $Q = 0$. When expanding against constant pressure, work is done by the gas ($W < 0$). By first law: $\Delta U = Q + W = 0 + W = W < 0$, so internal energy always decreases. Enthalpy change $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta (pV)$ is not necessarily constant.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. For adiabatic expansion of ideal gas, $\Delta U = W < 0$, so internal energy decreases.

question.question: To achieve cooling through throttling expansion, the condition that should be controlled for the throttling operation is:
options: D. There's no need to consider the value of $\mu$

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: The Joule-Thomson coefficient $\mu = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{H}$. For cooling ($\Delta T < 0$) during throttling expansion ($\Delta P < 0$), we need $\mu > 0$ so that $\Delta T = \mu \Delta P < 0$. Option A is correct.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. For cooling in throttling expansion, $\mu > 0$ is required.

question.question: For a fixed amount of ideal gas, from the same initial pressure $p_1$ reversibly expanded to pressure $p_2$, the relationship between the final volume of isothermal expansion and the final volume of adiabatic expansion is:
options: A. The former is greater than the latter
B. The former is smaller than the latter
C. There is no relationship between them

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: For isothermal expansion: $p_1V_1 = p_2V_{2,\text{iso}}$. For adiabatic expansion: $p_1V_1^\gamma = p_2V_{2,\text{adi}}^\gamma$. Since $\gamma > 1$, $V_{2,\text{adi}} < V_{2,\text{iso}}$ for the same pressure drop. So the isothermal final volume is greater than the adiabatic final volume.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Isothermal expansion results in larger final volume than adiabatic expansion for the same pressure change.

question.question: Starting from the definition $U = H - pV$, deduce which of the following relationships is incorrect:
options: A. $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial V}\right)_{p} - p$
B. $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = C_p \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{p} - p$
C. $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{p} \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{p} - T$
D. $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{p} \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{p} - p$

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: From $U = H - pV$, at constant p: $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial V}\right)_{p} - p$. Since $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{p} \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = C_p \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{p}$, we get $\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_{p} = C_p \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_{p} - p$. Option C incorrectly has -T instead of -p.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Option C has the wrong term (-T instead of -p).

question.question: Hess's law encompasses two important issues, namely:
options: A. The first law of thermodynamics and the third law of thermodynamics
B. The first law of thermodynamics and the basic nature of heat
C. The third law of thermodynamics and the basic nature of heat
D. The first law of thermodynamics and the basic characteristics of state functions

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Hess's law is based on two principles: (1) the first law of thermodynamics (energy conservation), and (2) the fact that enthalpy is a state function (path independence).
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Hess's law relies on the first law and the state function property of enthalpy.

question.question: When $\Delta_r C_{P,m} = 0$ for a chemical reaction, then $\Delta_r H_m(T)$ of the process with increasing temperature:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: From Kirchhoff's law: $\frac{d(\Delta H)}{dT} = \Delta C_P$. If $\Delta_r C_{P,m} = 0$, then $\frac{d(\Delta H)}{dT} = 0$, so $\Delta_r H_m(T)$ remains constant with temperature change.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. When $\Delta C_P = 0$, $\Delta H$ is independent of temperature.

question.question: Hydrogen and oxygen react in a 2:1 ratio in an adiabatic steel cylinder to form water. In this process:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: In an adiabatic rigid container (steel cylinder): $Q = 0$ and $W = 0$ (no volume change). By first law: $\Delta U = Q + W = 0 + 0 = 0$. $\Delta H$, $\Delta T$, and $\Delta P$ are not zero due to the chemical reaction.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. In adiabatic rigid container, $\Delta U = 0$ regardless of chemical reaction.

question.question: During a process with constant system temperature, between the system and surroundings:
options: B. Heat exchange must not occur
C. Heat exchange may or may not occur
D. Constant temperature is unrelated to heat exchange

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option C is correct. A system can maintain constant temperature through various means: adiabatic processes with work, or through heat exchange. Isothermal does not necessarily imply heat exchange (e.g., free expansion of ideal gas is isothermal with $Q=0$).
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Isothermal process doesn't necessarily involve heat exchange (e.g., free expansion of ideal gas).

question.question: At constant temperature and pressure, given the heat of reaction $\Delta H_1$ for $A \rightarrow 2B$ and $\Delta H_2$ for $2A \rightarrow C$, the heat of reaction $\Delta H_3$ for $C \rightarrow 4B$ is:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Using Hess's law: $C \rightarrow 4B$ is equivalent to reverse of $2A \rightarrow C$ plus 2 times $A \rightarrow 2B$: $\Delta H_3 = -\Delta H_2 + 2\Delta H_1 = 2\Delta H_1 - \Delta H_2$.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Application of Hess's law gives $\Delta H_3 = 2\Delta H_1 - \Delta H_2$.

question.question: At 298 K, the standard enthalpy of formation $\Delta H_{f,298}$ of graphite is:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: By definition, the standard enthalpy of formation of an element in its most stable form at 298 K and 1 bar is zero. Graphite is the most stable form of carbon at these conditions.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Standard enthalpy of formation of elements in their standard states is zero.

question.question: 1 mol of liquid benzene at 298 K is completely combusted in a bomb calorimeter, producing liquid water and carbon dioxide gas, while releasing 3264 kJ of heat. Then its constant pressure heat of combustion $Q_P$ is approximately:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: In bomb calorimeter (constant volume), $Q_V = -3264\ \mathrm{kJ}$ (heat released). For combustion: $C_6H_6(l) + \frac{15}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow 6CO_2(g) + 3H_2O(l)$, $\Delta n_g = 6 - 7.5 = -1.5$. $Q_P = Q_V + \Delta n_gRT = -3264 + (-1.5) \times 8.314 \times 298 / 1000 = -3264 - 3.72 = -3267.72\ \mathrm{kJ} \approx -3268\ \mathrm{kJ}$.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Conversion from constant volume to constant pressure heat of combustion gives approximately -3268 kJ.

question.question: Given the standard heats of formation $\Delta_f H_m$ (kJ·mol⁻¹) of $CH_3COOH(l)$, $CO_2(g)$, and $H_2O(l)$ are -484.5, -393.5, and -285.8 respectively, then the standard heat of combustion $\Delta_c H_m$ (kJ·mol⁻¹) of $CH_3COOH(l)$ is:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Combustion: $CH_3COOH(l) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow 2CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$. $\Delta_c H = 2\Delta_f H(CO_2) + 2\Delta_f H(H_2O) - \Delta_f H(CH_3COOH) - 2\Delta_f H(O_2) = 2(-393.5) + 2(-285.8) - (-484.5) - 0 = -787 - 571.6 + 484.5 = -874.1\ \mathrm{kJ\cdot mol^{-1}}$.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Calculation using standard enthalpies of formation gives -874.1 kJ·mol⁻¹.

question.question: Given the heat effect $\Delta H$ of the reaction $H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: A. $\Delta H$ is the heat of formation of $H_2O(l)$
B. $\Delta H$ is the heat of combustion of $H_2(g)$
C. $\Delta H$ is not equal to the numerical value of $\Delta U$ of the reaction
D. $\Delta H$ is equal to the numerical value of $\Delta H^0$

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option D is incorrect because $\Delta H$ is the enthalpy change for the reaction as written, but $\Delta H^0$ typically denotes the standard enthalpy change (at 1 bar and specified temperature). Without specifying standard conditions, they may not be equal. Options A, B, and C are correct: this is both the formation of water and combustion of hydrogen, and $\Delta H \neq \Delta U$ due to volume change.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation. Option D is incorrect as $\Delta H$ and $\Delta H^0$ may differ if not at standard conditions.

question.question: Given the heat effect $\Delta H$ of the reaction $C(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g)$, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: A. $\Delta H$ is the heat of formation of $CO_2(g)$
B. $\Delta H$ is the heat of combustion of $C(s)$
C. $\Delta H$ is not equal to the numerical value of $\Delta U$ of the reaction
D. $\Delta H$ is equal to the numerical value of $\Delta U$ of the reaction

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option D is incorrect. For $C(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g)$, $\Delta n_g = 0$, so $\Delta H = \Delta U$ (since $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta (pV) = \Delta U + \Delta n_g RT$ and $\Delta n_g = 0$). Options A and B are correct (this is both formation of CO₂ and combustion of carbon). Option C is incorrect because $\Delta H = \Delta U$ in this case.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation. Option D is incorrect because $\Delta H = \Delta U$ when $\Delta n_g = 0$.

question.question: Given the heat effect $\Delta H$ of the reaction $H_2(g) + \frac{1}{2} O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(g)$, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: A. $\Delta H$ is the heat of formation of $H_2O(g)$
B. $\Delta H$ is the heat of combustion of $H_2(g)$
D. $\Delta H$ is not equal to the numerical value of $\Delta U$

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Option B is incorrect because the heat of combustion of $H_2(g)$ is defined for formation of $H_2O(l)$, not $H_2O(g)$. Options A, C, and D are correct: this is the formation of water vapor (exothermic), and $\Delta H \neq \Delta U$ due to volume change ($\Delta n_g = -0.5$).
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Heat of combustion of hydrogen is for formation of liquid water, not vapor.

question.question: When using bond enthalpies to estimate the heat of reaction, the heat effect $\Delta H$ for the reaction $C_2H_6(g) \rightarrow C_2H_4(g) + H_2(g)$ is:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: $\Delta H = \sum (\text{bonds broken}) - \sum (\text{bonds formed})$. In $C_2H_6$: 1 C-C + 6 C-H bonds broken. In products: 1 C=C + 4 C-H + 1 H-H bonds formed. So $\Delta H = [E_{C-C} + 6E_{C-H}] - [E_{C=C} + 4E_{C-H} + E_{H-H}]$.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Option B correctly represents the bond enthalpy calculation.

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: Target: $C_2H_2 + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3CHO$. Using Hess's law: $C_2H_2 + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3CHO$ = (a) reverse + (d) reverse + (b) + 2×(c). $\Delta H = -226.8 + 1167 + (-286.0) + 2(-393.5) = -226.8 + 1167 - 286 - 787 = -132.8\ \mathrm{kJ\cdot mol^{-1}}$.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. Hess's law calculation gives -132.8 kJ·mol⁻¹.

question.question: If the enthalpy of stable elements in their standard states at temperature T is defined as zero, then the stipulated value of internal energy for stable elements will be:

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.solution: From $H = U + pV$, if $H = 0$ for elements, then $U = -pV$. Since $pV > 0$, $U < 0$. So the internal energy is always negative when enthalpy is defined as zero for elements.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_0_cleaned_.notes: Correct translation and answer. If H=0, then U=-pV<0.

question.question_stem: Using the Boltzmann distribution law, derive the energy formula for an independent particle system: $U = N k T^{2} \left(  rac{\partial \ln q}{\partial T} ight)_{N,V}$.
question.question: What is the derivation?
options: A. The derivation correctly uses Boltzmann distribution and partition function to show $U = N k T^2  rac{\partial \ln q}{\partial T}$.
B. The derivation assumes equal distribution of particles and incorrectly derives $U = N k T$.
C. The derivation uses classical mechanics and obtains $U =  rac{3}{2} N k T$ for monatomic gas.
D. The derivation is for dependent particles and gives a different formula.

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_1_cleaned_.solution: The correct derivation uses $n_i = N  rac{g_i e^{-\varepsilon_i / kT}}{q}$, then $U = \sum n_i \varepsilon_i =  rac{N}{q} \sum g_i \varepsilon_i e^{-\varepsilon_i / kT}$, and since $ rac{\partial q}{\partial T} =  rac{1}{k T^2} \sum g_i \varepsilon_i e^{-\varepsilon_i / kT}$, so $U = N k T^2  rac{1}{q}  rac{\partial q}{\partial T} = N k T^2  rac{\partial \ln q}{\partial T}$.

question.question_stem: Calculate at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 100  \mathrm{kPa}$ for NO molecule: translational partition function, translational energy, translational entropy, rotational partition function, rotational energy, rotational entropy, vibrational partition function, vibrational energy, vibrational entropy, total partition function, and molar internal energy and molar entropy of NO gas. Given NO molecular weight 30, moment of inertia $16.4 	imes 10^{-47}  \mathrm{kg} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}$, vibrational frequency $5.72 	imes 10^{13}  \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, ignore electronic and nuclear contributions.
question.question: What are the computed values?
options: A. All values are computed correctly using standard formulas.
B. Translational partition function is incorrect due to wrong volume calculation.
C. Vibrational entropy is overestimated because degeneracy is ignored.
D. Rotational energy is calculated using classical instead of quantum formula.

【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_1_cleaned_.solution: The detailed calculation involves: temperature T = 1273 K, pressure P = 100 kPa, molecular mass m = 30 / N_A kg, volume V = kT / P per molecule, translational partition function q_t = (2π m k T / h^2)^{3/2} V, translational energy U_t = (3/2) N k T, translational entropy S_t = N k [ln(q_t / N) + 5/2], rotational partition function q_r = 8π² I k T / h² for diatomic, rotational energy U_r = N k T, rotational entropy S_r = N k [ln q_r + 1], vibrational partition function q_v = 1 / (1 - e^{-hν / kT}), vibrational energy U_v = N k Θ_v / (e^{Θ_v / T} - 1) where Θ_v = hν / k, vibrational entropy S_v = N k [Θ_v / T / (e^{Θ_v / T} - 1) - ln(1 - e^{-Θ_v / T})], total partition function q = q_t q_r q_v, molar internal energy U = U_t + U_r + U_v, molar entropy S = S_t + S_r + S_v. Numerical values need to be computed based on given parameters.
【物理化学】核心教程第三版总复习题及答案_selected_1_cleaned_.notes: Comprehensive calculation requiring application of statistical thermodynamics formulas.

Atmospheric pressure does not affect the height, so D is correct.
【物理化学】（理）考研冲刺模拟题_selected_1_cleaned_4_1.notes: Correct application of the capillary height formula.

question.question_stem: Prove that $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = C_{p} + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial p}\right)_{T} \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial T}\right)_{V}$.
question.question: Proof:
options: A. The proof is valid as given in the solution.
C. An alternative proof is needed.

【物理化学】（理）考研冲刺模拟题_selected_1_cleaned_11_1.solution: Closed system, H is a function of T and p, i.e., H = f(T, p). Total differential: $dH = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{p} dT + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial p}\right)_{T} dp$. Keeping V constant, divide both sides by dT: $\left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_{p} + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial p}\right)_{T} \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial T}\right)_{V} = C_{p} + \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial p}\right)_{T} \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial T}\right)_{V}$. QED.
【物理化学】（理）考研冲刺模拟题_selected_1_cleaned_11_1.notes: Valid thermodynamic proof using total differentials.

question.question_stem: Argue that the adiabatic compressibility κ_S = - (1/V) (∂V/∂p)_S must be greater than zero for a homogeneous closed system. If the partial molar volume of substance B in solution V_B > 0, determine whether (∂μ_B/∂V)_{T,n} is greater than zero, less than zero, or equal to zero.
question.question: κ_S > 0; (∂μ_B/∂V)_{T,n} < 0

【物理化学】（理）考研冲刺模拟题_selected_1_cleaned_14_1.solution: For adiabatic compression, pressure increase leads to volume decrease, so (∂V/∂p)_S < 0, thus κ_S > 0. For (∂μ_B/∂V)_{T,n} = (∂μ_B/∂p)_{T,n} (∂p/∂V)_{T,n} = V_B (∂p/∂V)_{T,n}. Since V_B > 0 and (∂p/∂V)_{T,n} < 0 (pressure increases as volume decreases), (∂μ_B/∂V)_{T,n} < 0.
【物理化学】（理）考研冲刺模拟题_selected_1_cleaned_14_1.notes: Correct thermodynamic reasoning for compressibility and partial derivative.

question.question_stem: For the thermal decomposition of acetaldehyde with the given mechanism:
question.question: Derive the rate equation for methane generation.

【物理化学】（理）考研冲刺模拟题_selected_1_cleaned_15_1.solution: Using steady-state approximation for ·CH₃ and ·CH₂CHO: d[·CH₃]/dt = 0 gives k₁ [CH₃CHO] - 2k₄ [·CH₃]^2 = 0, so [·CH₃] = (k₁/(2k₄))^{1/2} [CH₃CHO]^{1/2}. Then d[CH₄]/dt = k₂ [·CH₃][CH₃CHO] = k₂ (k₁/(2k₄))^{1/2} [CH₃CHO]^{3/2}.
【物理化学】（理）考研冲刺模拟题_selected_1_cleaned_15_1.notes: Correct application of steady-state approximation to derive rate equations.
